summary: Julia Evans argues that asking questions, even about things you feel you 'should' already know, is one of the most powerful tools for learning. She shares how admitting she didn't understand what a Linux kernel does—despite using Linux for 10 years—led to deep learning and eventually giving talks on the topic. She highlights how even experts often don't know the answers to basic questions, citing Aphyr's discovery that RabbitMQ could lose 40% of data. The post advocates for saying 'I don't understand' openly, while being specific about what you do and don't know to avoid wasting anyone's time.
key_points: Asking questions instead of worrying about looking dumb unlocks actual learning
Even respected experts often don't know the answers to basic questions when you dig into specifics
Saying 'I don't understand' requires specifying exactly which part you don't understand to be effective
Being specific about your knowledge level prevents people from explaining things you already know
Understanding and learning are more important than feeling smart
money_quotes: I've started trying to ask questions any time I don't understand something, instead of worrying about whether people will think I'm dumb for not knowing it. This is magical, because it means I can then learn those things!
I'd been using Linux for 10 years, and I didn't really understand at all what the basic responsibilities of the Linux kernel were.
One surprising thing about asking questions is that when I start digging into a problem, people who I respect and who know a lot will sometimes not know the answers at all!
If you don't start asking questions about how RabbitMQ works from the beginning, then you'll never find that out.
Be skeptical! Don't believe what people say even if they're using fancy words!
If I don't tell them I don't understand, I'm never going to understand the damn thing.
Understanding and learning are more important than feeling smart.
key_insight: Admitting what you don't know and asking questions openly—even about things you feel you should already know—is the fastest path to genuine understanding.
